metadata.title: Why Angular Momentum Comes in a Ladder

source_of_truth: beat_sheet.json — edit the sheet, not this file

beats.label: This is Liam, in for Bear. One operator raises an angular-momentum projection by exactly h-bar; anot

beats.prompt: Choose simultaneous eigenstates of total angular momentum squared and its z projection. Label them j comma m. Total magnitude stays fixed by j, while m records
beats.label: Choose simultaneous eigenstates of total angular momentum squared and its z projection. Label them j

beats.prompt: Apply L-plus. If the result is nonzero, it has the same j and projection m plus one. Apply L-minus and the projection becomes m minus one. The operators move on
beats.label: Apply L-plus. If the result is nonzero, it has the same j and projection m plus one. Apply L-minus a

beats.prompt: Why not keep raising forever? A fixed vector magnitude cannot support an arbitrarily large z projection. Algebra says the same thing more sharply: the squared n
beats.label: Why not keep raising forever? A fixed vector magnitude cannot support an arbitrarily large z project

beats.prompt: The raised-state norm contains j times j plus one minus m times m plus one. The lowered-state norm contains j times j plus one minus m times m minus one. Neithe
beats.label: The raised-state norm contains j times j plus one minus m times m plus one. The lowered-state norm c

beats.prompt: At the top rung, raising must give zero. Setting that norm factor to zero makes the maximum projection m equal j. The ladder stops because there is no normaliza
beats.label: At the top rung, raising must give zero. Setting that norm factor to zero makes the maximum projecti

beats.prompt: At the bottom rung, lowering must give zero. The minimum projection is minus j. So the allowed values run from minus j to plus j in unit steps, for a total of t
beats.label: At the bottom rung, lowering must give zero. The minimum projection is minus j. So the allowed value

beats.prompt: Because the number of unit steps from minus j to plus j is two j, that number must be an integer. Ladder algebra therefore allows j equal zero, one half, one, t
beats.label: Because the number of unit steps from minus j to plus j is two j, that number must be an integer. La

beats.prompt: For orbital angular momentum, we rename j as ell. Single-valued spatial wavefunctions restrict ell to nonnegative integers. Half-integer values belong to spin o
beats.label: For orbital angular momentum, we rename j as ell. Single-valued spatial wavefunctions restrict ell t

beats.prompt: Once j is fixed, total magnitude is h-bar times the square root of j times j plus one. The projection ladder changes m, not that magnitude. Every rung is a diff
beats.label: Once j is fixed, total magnitude is h-bar times the square root of j times j plus one. The projectio

beats.prompt: Take orbital ell equals two. The allowed projections are minus two, minus one, zero, one, and two h-bar: five rungs. The magnitude is h-bar root six, larger tha
beats.label: Take orbital ell equals two. The allowed projections are minus two, minus one, zero, one, and two h-

beats.prompt: The ladder picture does not mean a hidden classical arrow jumps between literal tilts. It organizes eigenstates and measurement outcomes. Superpositions can com
beats.label: The ladder picture does not mean a hidden classical arrow jumps between literal tilts. It organizes

beats.prompt: Your turn. For j equals three halves, list the rungs. They are minus three halves, minus one half, plus one half, and plus three halves. Four states, matching t
beats.label: Your turn. For j equals three halves, list the rungs. They are minus three halves, minus one half, p

beats.prompt: Why does angular momentum come in a ladder? Raising and lowering shift projection by one, nonnegative norms force a top and bottom, and the endpoints quantize j
beats.label: Why does angular momentum come in a ladder? Raising and lowering shift projection by one, nonnegativ
